title: Project Artifacts & Governance
summary: The documents that authorise, control, and track a project - and the governance that keeps them aligned to value. Tailored to PMBOK 8, where Governance is now a full domain in its own right and artifacts provide the evidence behind the Governance and Scope domains.
intro: Charters, baselines, logs and change control - what each artifact is for.

sections.heading: Why artifacts and governance matter
sections.body: In PMBOK 8, artifacts are chosen to fit the project and are the evidence behind the domains they serve: the Scope domain (are we producing the right, accepted deliverables?) and the Monitoring & Controlling work that tells us whether we have reliable status to decide. Governance is now a full domain in its own right - nine processes covering integration, change control, knowledge, quality assurance, sourcing and close-out - and it is the authority framework that decides who approves what and how issues escalate.

blocks.title: How to read this guide
lines: Artifacts = the evidence: logs, registers, plans, baselines, reports.
Governance = the rules of the road: who decides, how issues escalate.
Monitoring & Controlling relies on status artifacts; the Scope domain relies on acceptance and change control.
Tailor the set to the project - there is no one mandatory list.

sections.heading: The project charter
sections.body: The charter formally authorises the project to exist and gives the project manager authority to apply organisational resources. Without it, the PM has no mandate. It is deliberately high-level and is created at the very start, before detailed planning.

rows.value: Project purpose and high-level objectives.
High-level scope and key deliverables.
Key milestones and an order-of-magnitude summary budget.
Key risks, assumptions and constraints.
Success criteria and the named sponsor.

blocks.title: Who signs it
lines: Signed or issued by the sponsor or initiator - someone external to the project with authority to fund it.
The PM is named in it, not the approver of it.

blocks.title: Trap
blocks.text: The PM does not approve the charter. The sponsor authorises it; the PM is named in it.

sections.heading: Governance & decision rights
sections.body: Governance is the framework of authority around the project: who makes which decisions, who is accountable, how issues escalate, and how the project connects to the sponsor, steering committee or board, and the wider organisation. Good governance gives clear accountability and a defined path for resolving issues and approving changes, so decisions are made at the right level rather than stalling or being made by the wrong people. Governance is the rules of the road; management is the day-to-day driving. The PM operates within the decision rights that governance defines.

blocks.title: Remember
lines: Governance sets WHO decides and HOW issues escalate - not the work itself.
The PM works within, not above, the decision rights governance defines.

sections.heading: Phase-gate (stage-gate) reviews
sections.body: Predictive and hybrid projects are often split into phases separated by gates. A gate is a formal checkpoint, not just a milestone. At each gate, leadership reviews the completed deliverables and re-tests whether the business case is still valid, then makes a decision before more money is committed. Gates control investment incrementally and keep the project aligned to expected value - if the business case has collapsed, the gate is where you stop spending.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='10' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='38' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 1</text><rect x='106' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='134' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 2</text><rect x='202' y='52' width='56' height='34' rx='4' stroke='#1F5FA8' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='230' y='73' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Phase 3</text><line x1='66' y1='69' x2='74' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='98' y1='69' x2='106' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='162' y1='69' x2='170' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='194' y1='69' x2='202' y2='69' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><polygon points='86,56 98,69 86,82 74,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='182,56 194,69 182,82 170,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><polygon points='278,56 290,69 278,82 266,69' fill='none' stroke='#B5462F' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='86' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='182' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='278' y='102' fill='#B5462F' font-size='8' text-anchor='middle'>gate</text><text x='150' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>each gate: go / hold / recycle / kill</text></svg>
figure.caption: Phases (boxes) are separated by gates (diamonds) where the project is continued or stopped.

items.title: Go
lines: Deliverables sound and business case valid - continue and commit the next tranche.

items.title: Hold
lines: Pause and revisit before committing more.

items.title: Recycle
lines: Send back for rework, then re-review.

items.title: Kill
lines: Business case no longer holds - stop spending.

blocks.title: A gate asks two questions
lines: Are the deliverables sound?
Is the business case still worth funding?

sections.heading: Artifacts by type -> purpose
sections.body: PMBOK 8 groups artifacts by type rather than by knowledge area. Recognise which family an artifact belongs to and what it is for. PMI keeps RAID items as separate artifacts even though the real world often consolidates them into a single RAID log.

blocks.title: Logs and registers -> track ongoing items

rows.value: Future, uncertain events; probability, impact, response, owner.
Problems happening now that need action.
Assumptions and constraints being relied on.
Who stakeholders are and their interest/influence.
Requested changes and their disposition.

blocks.title: Plans -> define how work is done

rows.value: How each aspect (scope, schedule, cost, quality, etc.) is managed.
How to engage stakeholders (vs the register, which lists who they are).
Approved scope statement, WBS and WBS dictionary.
Approved schedule to measure against.
Approved time-phased budget to measure against.
Feed Monitoring & Controlling with reliable, actionable status.
Authorises the project (see above).
Justifies the investment; re-tested at each gate.
Decomposes scope - covered in the Schedule & Network Diagrams guide.

blocks.title: Dependencies
blocks.text: Dependencies are tracked within the schedule, not a standalone PMI artifact - the D in a RAID log.

sections.heading: RAID: risk vs issue
sections.body: A RAID log is a single consolidated view of Risks, Assumptions, Issues and Dependencies, used in the real world to keep all four visible in one place. For the exam, know the nuance: PMI formally keeps these as separate artifacts (risk register, assumption log, issue log, and dependencies in the schedule). Treat RAID as a practical consolidation, but recognise the individual PMI documents. The two most often confused are risk and issue.

groups.label: Definition
lines: A future, uncertain event that may or may not happen.

groups.label: Lives in
lines: The risk register.

groups.label: Definition
lines: Something already happening now that needs action.

groups.label: Lives in
lines: The issue log.

blocks.title: Don't merge them
blocks.text: Risk = future uncertainty (risk register). Issue = happening now (issue log).

sections.heading: Baseline vs register updates
sections.body: The single most important governance distinction for artifacts: registers and logs are updated freely as new information arrives, but a baseline is the approved yardstick and can only change through the formal change-control process.

rows.value: Updated freely and continuously - add risks, issues, assumptions, stakeholders as they emerge. No change request needed.
Approved reference for scope, schedule or cost. Changing it requires an approved change request.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Never edit a baseline directly.
Baseline change -> raise a change request; governance/CCB approves before the baseline moves.
Updating a register does NOT need a change request.

sections.heading: The change-control path
sections.body: When a change touches an approved baseline, follow the governed process rather than acting directly. This keeps authority, value and accountability connected.

items.title: Capture the request
lines: Log it in the change log; describe the change and its trigger.

items.title: Assess the impact
lines: Evaluate effect on scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk and value.

items.title: Route for decision
lines: Take it to the approver defined by governance (e.g. CCB or sponsor).

items.title: Update baselines if approved
lines: Only after approval, re-baseline and communicate the new reference.

items.title: Trace it back
lines: Link the change to the business case and charter so value stays connected.

blocks.title: Governance principle
blocks.text: Decisions are made at the right level; escalate only when the normal path cannot resolve it.

sections.heading: How the artifacts work together
sections.body: These documents form a chain of authority and control. The business case justifies the work and the charter authorises it; governance defines who may decide what; gates re-confirm value at set points; and the RAID artifacts keep risks, assumptions, issues and dependencies under continuous control. Scope is decomposed separately in the Work Breakdown Structure - see the Schedule & Network Diagrams guide for the WBS in full. Every change request and decision should be traceable back through this structure, so authority, value and accountability stay connected from start to close.

items.title: Business case
lines: Justifies the investment.

items.title: Charter
lines: Authorises the project and the PM.

items.title: Governance
lines: Decides who approves what.

items.title: Gates
lines: Confirm value before committing more.

items.title: RAID + baselines
lines: Keep risks and the approved yardstick under control.

blocks.title: One-line recall
blocks.text: Business case justifies, charter authorises, governance decides, gates confirm value - and baselines only move by change request.

sections.heading: Acceptance criteria
sections.body:
Acceptance criteria state the measurable conditions a deliverable must meet before stakeholders will accept it as complete. They turn vague expectations into testable checks and reduce disputes at handoff.

Write them early, ideally when requirements are agreed. Poor criteria show up late as rework, scope arguments, and delayed formal acceptance.
sections.tip: If a scenario asks whether work is done, look for acceptance criteria and who is authorized to accept the deliverable.

groups.label: What
lines: Conditions you can verify with a pass or fail result.

groups.label: Use
lines: Avoid soft wording like better or user-friendly unless you define how it will be measured.

groups.label: What
lines: A shared understanding between the team and the accepting stakeholder.

groups.label: Use
lines: Confirm criteria before build and re-check them before formal acceptance.

groups.label: What
lines: Only the work required for that deliverable, nothing extra.

groups.label: Use
lines: Keeps acceptance aligned to the scope baseline and reduces gold-plating.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Acceptance criteria support the Scope domain: they define done for a deliverable. Changing them after agreement is a scope change, not an informal team tweak.

sections.heading: Subsidiary management plans
sections.body:
The project management plan is the integrated master document. Subsidiary plans explain how each aspect of the work will be planned, executed, monitored, and controlled.

On the exam, treat these plans as governance scaffolding: they set the agreed way of working before the team improvises under pressure.

rows.value: How scope is defined, validated, and controlled; links to WBS and acceptance.
How activities are sequenced, estimated, baselined, and tracked.
How costs are estimated, budgeted, funded, and controlled against the cost baseline.
Quality standards, assurance activities, and control methods for deliverables.
How people, equipment, and materials are planned, acquired, and managed.
Who needs what information, how often, in what format, and through which channel.
How risks are identified, analyzed, responded to, and monitored.
What will be bought or contracted, how sellers are selected, and how contracts are managed.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Integration management coordinates these pieces. When objectives compete (scope vs cost vs time vs quality), the plan and change process define how trade-offs are decided.

sections.heading: Progress and schedule visuals
sections.body: Visual status artifacts help stakeholders see progress without reading every task. Pick the chart that matches the delivery model and the question you need answered.
sections.tip: Match the visual to the method: Gantt for predictive timelines; burndown, burnup, and CFD for iterative or flow work.

groups.label: What
lines: Timeline bars for activities, durations, dependencies, and milestones.

groups.label: Use
lines: Show planned vs actual schedule in predictive or hybrid plans.

groups.label: What
lines: Work remaining over time, often against an ideal completion line.

groups.label: Use
lines: Track whether a sprint or fixed set of work will finish on time.

groups.label: What
lines: Work completed over time plus a total-scope line.

groups.label: Use
lines: Show progress and whether scope has grown while work completes.

groups.label: What
lines: Stacked bands of work items by workflow state over time.

groups.label: Use
lines: Spot bottlenecks, rising WIP, and unstable flow in Kanban-style systems.

items.text: Remaining work falls toward zero; best when scope for the period is fairly stable.
Completed work rises; total-scope line makes scope change visible.
Band width shows inventory in each state; widening in-progress bands signal flow problems.

sections.heading: Quality analysis tools
sections.body: Quality governance needs evidence, not opinions. Two high-yield tools help separate process noise from real problems and dig to root causes when defects or delays appear.

groups.label: What
lines: Data plotted over time with a center line, upper control limit, and lower control limit.

groups.label: Use
lines: Decide if a process is stable (common-cause variation) or out of control (special-cause variation).

groups.label: What
lines: Cause-and-effect diagram that groups possible causes under categories such as people, process, tools, materials, environment, and management.

groups.label: Use
lines: Structure root-cause analysis for a known problem, defect, or effect before jumping to fixes.

rows.value: Define standards, metrics, and how quality will be built in.
Process-focused assurance that the approach can meet the standards.
Inspect deliverables and measurements; control charts often live here.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: A point outside control limits (or a non-random pattern) means investigate special causes. Do not redesign the whole process for normal random variation.

sections.heading: Communications as a governed artifact
sections.body:
Communication failure is a top project killer. The communications management plan is the artifact that turns stakeholder needs into a repeatable information system rather than ad hoc messages.

It sits under governance because reporting cadence, escalation paths, and decision forums only work when people know who says what, to whom, and when.

items.title: Audience and need
lines: Which stakeholders need which information, and why.

items.title: Format and channel
lines: Report, meeting, dashboard, message, or workshop; push vs pull vs interactive.

items.title: Frequency and owner
lines: How often communication happens and who is responsible for sending it.

items.title: Escalation
lines: When issues leave the normal path and move to sponsor, steering group, or CCB.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: The stakeholder register lists who people are. The communications (and stakeholder engagement) plans describe how you inform and involve them. Do not mix those artifacts.

sections.heading: Decision trees
sections.body:
A decision tree maps choices, chance events, and outcomes so teams can compare alternatives with expected value or preferred risk exposure. It is useful when a governance decision has several paths and uncertain payoffs.

Treat it as analysis input for decision-makers, not as the decision itself. The tree clarifies options; governance still decides.

items.text: A point where the team or sponsor chooses among options (often shown as a square).
An uncertain event with probabilities and branch outcomes (often a circle).
End result of a path, usually with cost, benefit, or net value.

items.title: Frame the decision
lines: State the choice and the objective (cost, schedule recovery, risk reduction, value).

items.title: Branch options and uncertainties
lines: List alternatives, then add chance events that change results.

items.title: Score the paths
lines: Attach costs, benefits, and probabilities; compute expected monetary value where useful.

items.title: Recommend and route
lines: Present the preferred path to the authority defined by governance.

blocks.title: Exam tip
blocks.text: Decision trees often appear with expected monetary value (EMV). EMV = probability x impact for each branch, then choose the best overall path for the decision node.

source: PM Cram — aligned to the current PMBOK Guide and PMI Examination Content Outline.
